definition: A linked list is a linear collection of nodes, where each node holds a value and a pointer (or pointers) to the next node. Memory is not contiguous, so random access is O(n), but inserting or removing at a known position is O(1).
layman: A chain of paper notes, each pointing to the next note in the chain. To find note #5 you have to walk from the first one, but cutting and re-stapling is easy.
content:
## Linked lists

A **singly-linked list** stores items as nodes:

```
[ value | next ] -> [ value | next ] -> [ value | None ]
```

A **doubly-linked list** also stores a backward pointer:

```
[ prev | value | next ] <-> [ prev | value | next ] <-> [ prev | value | None ]
```

### Complexity (vs Python list)

| Operation | Linked list | Python list (array) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Access by index `lst[i]` | O(n) — walk from head | O(1) |
| Insert/remove at known node | **O(1)** | O(n) (must shift) |
| Insert/remove at head | **O(1)** | O(n) |
| Insert/remove at tail (with tail pointer) | O(1) | O(1) amortized |
| Memory locality | poor (random heap addresses) | excellent (contiguous) |

### When linked lists actually win

- You need cheap **insert/remove anywhere in the middle** when you already hold a node reference.
- You need **O(1) at both ends** without occasional resize copies — `collections.deque` is implemented as a doubly-linked list of memory blocks.
- You're implementing an **LRU cache** with `OrderedDict` (which uses a doubly-linked list internally) or your own LRU node graph.

### When linked lists lose

For almost everything else in Python, an **array-backed list** beats a hand-rolled linked list because:

- CPython lists are written in C and exploit CPU cache lines.
- Random access (`lst[i]`) is O(1) instead of O(n).
- Memory overhead per element is far lower (no extra `next` pointer per node).

### Why this matters for Gen AI

You'll rarely write a linked list yourself, but you **use** them constantly:

- `collections.deque` for streaming buffers, BFS worklists, request queues.
- `OrderedDict` (and therefore LRU caches like `functools.lru_cache`) for embedding caches, response caches and retrieval result caches.
- A retrieval result "chain" produced by a graph traversal — each step points to the next.
keyPoints: Linked list = nodes pointing to other nodes; not contiguous in memory.
Random access is O(n); insert/remove at a known node is O(1).
Doubly-linked allows O(1) removal from anywhere with a node reference — basis of LRU caches.
In Python you almost always use deque or OrderedDict instead of rolling your own.
Trade locality (Python list) for cheap-middle-edits (linked list) only when it actually matters.
